title: Creation / Adam

alignment_rows.summary: All three traditions frame Adam within a divine speech-act, but the narrative entry point differs (cosmic creation sequence vs. angelic announcement).

notes.title: Narrative entry
notes.detail: Adam appears after an ordered creation progression.

notes.title: Narrative entry
notes.detail: Shares Genesis sequence placement, then extended by later NT intertextual use.

notes.title: Narrative entry
notes.detail: Adam begins with divine declaration to angels and material/origin statements across surahs.

alignment_rows.summary: Each corpus marks humans as specially formed; wording clusters differ (image-language in Genesis; clay/dust motifs in Qur'an).

notes.title: Wording profile
notes.detail: Uses image/likeness and breath-of-life language in two adjacent creation presentations.

notes.title: Wording profile
notes.detail: Retains Genesis phrasing with translation variation by English edition.

notes.title: Wording profile
notes.detail: Reiterates clay-origin and divine breath motifs in recurring formulaic diction.

alignment_rows.summary: All three place the first humans in a provisioned setting with a delegated role; role vocabulary differs (dominion/till-keep vs khalifah frame).

notes.title: Role terms
notes.detail: Mandate language emphasizes fruitfulness and tending/guarding the garden.

notes.title: Role terms
notes.detail: Uses the same Genesis role terms in canonical Old Testament form.

notes.title: Role terms
notes.detail: Frames human role through khalifah language and garden residence command.

alignment_rows.summary: A concrete prohibition structures the plot in each tradition, though the exact command wording and warning sequence vary.

notes.title: Command form
notes.detail: Direct command includes a consequence clause linked to a named tree.

notes.title: Command form
notes.detail: Parallel command syntax with translation-level differences in modality.

notes.title: Command form
notes.detail: Tree prohibition appears with warning language linked to enmity/deception context.

alignment_rows.summary: All include a deception-to-eating sequence; actor labeling and dialogue staging differ across corpora.

notes.title: Sequence
notes.detail: Serpent dialogue precedes woman/man eating in a tightly narrated scene.

notes.title: Sequence
notes.detail: Mirrors Genesis sequence and wording structure by translation tradition.

notes.title: Sequence
notes.detail: Iblis/Shaytan whispering motif is distributed across retellings with compressed scene movement.

alignment_rows.summary: All three narrate removal from prior ease followed by continued divine address; explicit repentance formula is most foregrounded in Qur'anic passages.

notes.title: Aftermath structure
notes.detail: Judgment, clothing, and expulsion form a single narrative arc.

notes.title: Aftermath structure
notes.detail: Maintains Genesis arc; later Christian texts often echo Adam typology beyond this scene.

notes.title: Aftermath structure
notes.detail: Descent to earth is paired with accepted repentance and future-guidance promise.

similarities: Adam's account is initiated by divine speech and intention rather than human action.
A specific prohibition functions as the central narrative boundary.
Deception precedes disobedience in plot sequence.
Post-transgression life continues under divine address/guidance.

differences.description: Torah/Tanakh and Christian Bible preserve a contiguous Eden plotline in Genesis 2-3, while Qur'anic material appears as distributed retellings across multiple surahs.
Actor labeling differs: Genesis uses 'serpent' in-scene; Qur'anic passages identify Iblis/Shaytan as deceiver in corresponding episodes.
Key role terms (e.g., dominion/stewardship; khalifah) and consequence language vary by translation choices, affecting perceived emphasis without altering core sequence.
Repentance is explicitly verbalized in Qur'an 2:37/20:122 sequence, whereas Genesis 3 emphasizes judgment-exile progression with implied mercy motifs.

citations.note: Narrative structure and sequence anchors for creation-to-exile arc.
Shared Genesis storyline used for wording and sequence comparison.
Distributed Adam episodes used to map cross-surah sequence and thematic parallels.

commentary_notes: Scope constrained to narrative structure, sequence, wording profiles, thematic parallels, and explicit differences.
This node intentionally avoids theological adjudication or harmonization claims.
Schema is reusable for future SpiritOS nodes via alignment_rows and source_metadata.citations arrays.
